prompts.prompt: We're 70% through our cloud migration but the business says they don't see any value. IT says things are going great. What's happening?
prompts.reason: This is the canonical value-gap scenario. Technical migration has outpaced organizational change; proxy metrics show progress; business outcomes show none.

prompts.prompt: Our board presentation shows we've migrated 847 of 1,200 servers to the cloud. Is this the right thing to show the board?
prompts.reason: Server count is a proxy metric. The skill provides the CTRL-F test and explains why this metric does not communicate business progress to a board audience.

prompts.prompt: We had a slow start to our cloud migration but we're planning to deliver most of the value in the second half. Is this realistic?
prompts.reason: This is the hockey-stick fallacy. The skill explains why deferred-value claims almost never materialize and what the realistic S-curve expectation looks like.

prompts.prompt: How should we sequence which applications to migrate first — easiest ones to build momentum, or something else?
prompts.reason: Sequencing by technical convenience (easiest first) is one of the levers the skill addresses. The correct approach is to sequence by value contribution, even if technically harder.

prompts.prompt: Business stakeholders are losing confidence in our cloud program. Our instinct is to accelerate the migration to show more progress. Is that the right response?
prompts.reason: Accelerating technical migration to address eroding business support is exactly the wrong response if a value gap exists. The skill explains that the constraint is organizational, not technical.

prompts.prompt: What metrics should we use to measure cloud migration success in our quarterly business review?
prompts.reason: The skill provides the explicit framework: distinguish IT proxy metrics from business value metrics, define the transition from one to the other as migration matures.

prompts.prompt: We need to decide whether to migrate our monolith to containers or rewrite it as microservices. What are the trade-offs?
prompts.reason: This is a migration strategy / application architecture question, not a metrics or value-gap question.

prompts.prompt: Our enterprise cloud platform requires all developers to submit a ticket to get a new environment provisioned. Is this a problem?
prompts.reason: This is an Enterprise Non-Cloud diagnostic question (on-demand self-service violated), not a migration metrics / value gap question.

prompts.prompt: We want to avoid cloud vendor lock-in. What's the best approach?
prompts.reason: This is a lock-in cost optimization / ROL question, not a migration metrics question.

prompts.prompt: Our team writes cloud strategy principles but they all seem obvious and no one debates them. How do we make better principles?
prompts.reason: This is a principles quality checklist question (opposite test, memorability). Not a value-gap question.

prompts.prompt: We migrated to the cloud primarily to vacate an expiring data center contract. We met the deadline and moved 100% of workloads. Is that success?
prompts.reason: Boundary case: the skill's boundary section explicitly notes that cost-avoidance or contract-expiry migrations have cost and completion as legitimate primary metrics, not just proxy metrics. But the skill's value framing still applies if the organization claims broader benefits beyond contract compliance.

prompts.prompt: Our cloud program has been running for 18 months. We have great deployment automation and 90% of servers migrated. But customer satisfaction scores haven't changed. Should we be worried?
prompts.reason: This involves both the value gap (customer satisfaction unchanged despite strong IT metrics) and the question of whether customer satisfaction is a lagging metric with a longer feedback cycle. The skill applies to name the value gap, but the boundary section's note about lagging indicators is also relevant here.
